questions.prompt: What does Continuous Integration do for a test suite?
options: It writes the tests for you
It runs the suite automatically on every push or pull request, on a clean machine, so breaks are caught immediately
It only runs tests once a month
questions.explanation: CI runs the suite for you on every push or PR, on a fresh machine, the same way each time, so a regression is caught at once instead of days later.

questions.prompt: Why must browser tests run headless on a CI runner?
options: The runner has no screen, so headless runs the real browser engine with no visible window
It is the only mode Selenium supports
questions.explanation: A CI server has no display. Headless runs the genuine browser engine without a window, so the same tests work on the server and run faster too.

questions.prompt: Why upload reports and screenshots as artifacts with `if: always()`?
options: To make the workflow file shorter
So the report is saved even when tests fail, letting you download and diagnose the break without rerunning locally
Artifacts are only kept when the build succeeds otherwise
questions.explanation: You most need the report when the suite fails. `if: always()` ensures it is uploaded even on failure, so you can diagnose from the downloaded artifact instead of rerunning.

questions.prompt: What does fail-fast mean in a CI pipeline?
options: Run every step no matter what, then report at the end
Stop the pipeline as soon as a critical step fails, so a broken build blocks the merge quickly instead of wasting minutes
Retry each failing step five times
Skip the test step to finish faster
questions.explanation: Fail-fast halts the pipeline the moment a critical step fails. A failed test build should block the merge and report quickly rather than churning through later steps.
